serving.description: 1 bowl (~650g total: pork ribs with bone ~150g, lotus root ~80g, corn ~80g, peanuts ~20g, red dates ~15g, wolfberry ~5g, broth ~300ml)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard home-style serving is approximately 650g, comprising pork ribs with bone (~150g), sliced fresh lotus root (~80g), half a corn cob (~80g), raw peanuts (~20g), three red dates (~15g), wolfberry (~5g), and approximately 300ml of slow-simmered broth.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Rib Lotus Root Soup — component-based calculation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for pork ribs, lotus root, peanuts, corn, red dates, and wolfberry, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore nutrient database for composite soup estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) + Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for this exact composite dish. Values are derived from weighted component calculation: pork ribs (USDA SR Legacy #10978), lotus root (USDA SR Legacy #11167), peanuts (USDA SR Legacy #16087), sweet corn (USDA SR Legacy #11167), red dates (USDA SR Legacy #09087), wolfberry/goji (USDA SR Legacy #09163), adjusted for 2-hour simmering water absorption and bone weight exclusion. Cross-referenced with HPB Singapore soup nutrient benchmarks.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from analogous foods: lotus root (GI ~38, partial resistant starch), peanuts (GI ~14), pork (GI ~0), corn (GI ~52) — weighted composite estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database entries for lotus root and peanuts
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite soup. GI ~40 is a weighted analogous estimate: lotus root GI ~38 (University of Sydney GI Database, partial resistant starch), peanuts GI ~14 (University of Sydney GI Database), corn GI ~52 (University of Sydney GI Database), pork ribs GI ~0 (protein/fat, no carbohydrate contribution). Weighted by carbohydrate contribution per serving: lotus root ~8g, corn ~8g, peanuts ~3g, red dates ~3g. Composite GI ~40, GL ~8 per 650g bowl (22g total carbohydrate). Confidence: 0.85.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~65mg per 100g; ~425mg per 650g bowl) — prepared without soy sauce or added salt beyond a minimal finishing pinch
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 425mg sodium per bowl (well below the 600mg per-meal target for hypertensive individuals), this soup is one of the lowest-sodium Chinese home soups available and is suitable for regular consumption by people managing hypertension, provided no additional salt or soy sauce is added at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and low glycemic load (~8 per bowl); lotus root provides partial resistant starch that attenuates postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI (~40) and low GL (~8) of this soup make it suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes; the partial resistant starch in lotus root slows glucose absorption, and the high protein and fat content from pork ribs and peanuts further blunts postprandial glycaemic response — total carbohydrate per bowl is approximately 22g, which is manageable within a standard diabetic meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from pork ribs (~6–7g per bowl); partially offset by MUFA from peanuts and anti-inflammatory wolfberry zeaxanthin
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this soup in moderation: saturated fat from pork ribs (~6–7g per bowl) approaches one-third of the recommended daily limit; however, the absence of added oils, low sodium, high potassium (~748mg per bowl), and anti-inflammatory compounds from wolfberry and lotus root provide partial cardiovascular benefit — trimming visible fat from ribs before serving is recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory wolfberry zeaxanthin (~9mg per bowl) and Vitamin C from lotus root (~39mg per bowl) support joint health and collagen synthesis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This soup is suitable for people with rheumatoid arthritis; wolfberry zeaxanthin provides anti-inflammatory and eye-protective benefits, Vitamin C from lotus root supports collagen synthesis relevant to joint integrity, and peanuts contribute MUFA and trace Vitamin E with mild anti-inflammatory properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Rib and Lotus Root Soup (莲藕排骨汤, Lián'ǒu Páigǔ Tāng) is a classic Cantonese slow-boiled soup (lao huo tang) widely prepared in Singapore and Malaysian Chinese households. One standard bowl (~650g) contains pork ribs with bone (~150g), sliced fresh lotus root (~80g), half a corn cob (~80g), raw peanuts (~20g), three red dates (~15g), wolfberry/goji berries (~5g), and approximately 300ml of slow-simmered broth. The soup is simmered for 2 hours over low heat without added oil, producing a clear, lightly sweet, and mildly savoury broth. Lotus root contributes a tender, slightly crunchy texture and earthy sweetness; peanuts become soft and creamy; pork ribs provide body and umami depth. Nutritionally, one bowl delivers approximately 410 kcal, 26g protein, 20g fat (6–7g saturated), 22g carbohydrate, 5g dietary fibre, and only ~425mg sodium — making it one of the lowest-sodium Chinese soups available. The glycemic index is low (~40) and glycemic load is low (~8), supported by partial resistant starch in lotus root and the protein-fat matrix from pork and peanuts. Key micronutrients include Vitamin C (~39mg, 65% of daily AI), potassium (~748mg), iron (~3.3mg), and wolfberry zeaxanthin (~9mg for eye health). In Traditional Chinese Medicine, this soup is valued for nourishing the blood, strengthening the spleen, and promoting vitality. It is commonly served as a family weekly soup, postpartum recovery soup, and restorative meal during illness. Suitable for people managing hypertension (low sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (low GI/GL), and rheumatoid arthritis. Moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from pork ribs. Gluten-free and dairy-free. Not halal (contains pork). Source: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy + HPB Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from weighted component calculation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for all major ingredients (pork ribs, lotus root, peanuts, corn, red dates, wolfberry), cross-referenced with HPB Singapore soup benchmarks. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are supported by approved sources. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for lotus root and peanuts with documented weighting methodology. Confidence is high given the well-characterised ingredient list and established approved-source coverage.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite slow-boiled soup are not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Singapore database as of 2026-04-14; total fat (3.1g/100g) and saturated fat (1.1g/100g) are confirmed from approved sources but MUFA and PUFA sub-fractions for this specific composite preparation are not available. water_g is set to -1: precise moisture content for this slow-simmered composite soup (with variable broth absorption during 2-hour cooking) is not reported as a single value in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Singapore database as of 2026-04-14; moisture varies with cooking duration and serving ratio of solids to broth. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this home-cooked preparation without hydrogenated fats per USDA SR Legacy pork rib and peanut entries. GI ~40 is an analogous estimate weighted from University of Sydney GI Database entries for lotus root (~38), peanuts (~14), and corn (~52); no direct GI measurement exists for this composite soup. Dietary flag is_halal set to false: dish contains pork and is not halal. FoodEx2 code A0JKG used as closest defensible parent (pork rib vegetable soup); no exact code exists for this specific Cantonese preparation. Vitamin A reported as 20 mcg RAE per 100g, sourced from wolfberry and red dates component contributions. Folate reported as 10 mcg DFE per 100g from lotus root and peanut contributions per USDA SR Legacy.
